decomposition.title: Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
decomposition.industry: Manufacturing / consumer products / hospitality / enterprise IT
decomposition.estimated_deal_size_usd: No explicit budget or current spend stated. Inferred opportunity size is approximately $15M-$50M+ over an assumed 3-5 year hosting transformation horizon, based on enterprise-scale data center exit scope, large VMware estate, significant physical server footprint, complex SAP ecosystem, DR/HA, networking, security, monitoring, and possible migration/managed services costs. Phase 0 advisory/evaluation work alone would likely be materially smaller.
decomposition.contract_duration: No explicit contract duration stated. The RFP is a Phase 0 evaluation and says detailed terms will be finalized in subsequent phases; inferred future hosting/business-case horizon is likely 3-5 years for Cloud/DCaaS/Hybrid services.

requirements.text: Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing target architecture and platform capabilities, high-level transition approach and indicative sequencing, operating model and support framework, and commercial and pricing structure.
Proposed solutions should support enterprise-scale deployments, secure connectivity, and integration with Kohler’s enterprise and manufacturing ecosystem.
The evaluation should ensure application performance and availability are maintained or improved.
The evaluation should ensure integration with manufacturing and enterprise systems is supported.
The evaluation should ensure transition risks and business disruption are minimized.

Vendors are expected to propose a target operating model including roles and responsibilities, shared responsibility framework, governance, security, compliance approach, and operations and support model.
Vendors must clearly articulate target-state architecture aligned to multi-account / multi-subscription landing zone principles.
Vendors must clearly articulate the landing zone deployment model and application deployment model.
Vendors must address environment segregation for Prod / Test / Dev.
Vendors must address identity integration with enterprise identity providers.
Vendors must address the hybrid connectivity approach.
Vendors must address region strategy and data residency approach.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Data residency appears as one bullet inside the SOW, with no country-specific rule stated; this is a common location for sovereignty constraints in companion templates or later clarifications.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Region strategy and data residency approach required; no explicit jurisdiction, residency country, or offshore-processing prohibition stated in this document.

requirements.text: Vendors must address disaster recovery, high availability and scalability patterns.
Architecture diagrams, reference designs and supporting documentation are strongly encouraged.
Vendors must respond for relevant scenarios (Cloud, DCaaS, Hybrid) where applicable and clearly state assumptions.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The requirement applies only where relevant and requires assumptions to be explicit; vendors could lose comparability if they skip a hosting scenario without explanation.

requirements.text: In-scope services include enterprise applications currently hosted in the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Current core hosting environment is the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center.

requirements.text: In-scope services include commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) and internally developed applications.
In-scope services include Windows and Linux virtual machines.
In-scope services include databases including SQL Server, Oracle, SAP HANA, MySQL, PostgreSQL and other supported platforms.
In-scope services include middleware and integration platforms.
In-scope services include file storage systems and shared infrastructure services.
In-scope services include backup and disaster recovery solutions.
In-scope services include identity and access management integrations.
In-scope services include network connectivity services across enterprise and manufacturing environments.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Connectivity must cover enterprise and manufacturing environments.

requirements.text: In-scope services include monitoring, logging and security tooling.
In-scope services include selected OT-adjacent workloads, limited to read-only or controlled integration scenarios.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: OT-adjacent workloads are included but limited; exceeding read-only or controlled integration scenarios could create scope and risk issues.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applies to manufacturing/OT-adjacent environments only as limited by the RFP.

requirements.text: Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards. Evidence of certifications required.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This mandatory certification evidence requirement is buried in the Compliance & Regulatory Requirements section and could be missed if relying only on the scope summary.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applies to applicable industry/regional standards relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.
requirements.cert_required: Evidence of certifications required for applicable industry/regional standards; specific certifications are not named in the main RFP.

requirements.text: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is a provider-type-specific mandatory requirement in the compliance subsection and refers to an external template section not included in the text.
requirements.cert_required: DCaaS providers must provide compliance attestations as per industry standards; detailed information is stated to be in the Vendor response template Section 4.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to review, validate, and clearly state any deviations or additional assumptions in their proposal.
Vendors are expected to propose Cloud/DCaaS and/or Hybrid hosting solutions aligned to Kohler’s stated objectives and constraints.
Vendors are expected to base their response on the provided inventory dataset.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The inventory dataset is external to the main document; inability to access or use it would impair sizing, solution design, and pricing compliance.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to highlight any data gaps or assumptions made due to incomplete information.
Vendors are expected to use the provided inventory as the baseline for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The provided inventory is the required baseline; alternate sizing assumptions must be transparent to remain comparable.

requirements.text: Where data gaps exist, vendors must clearly state assumptions used for sizing and costing.
Vendors are required to submit structured, complete, and comparable proposals aligned to the requirements outlined in this RFP and the associated response templates.
Vendors must clearly state all assumptions, dependencies, and constraints associated with their response.
Any optional services or additional capabilities not covered under the scope must be explicitly identified separately.
Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Submission timing and designated communication channel are procedural requirements that can exclude a bid before technical evaluation if missed.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple ‘Yes/No’ answers will not be considered sufficient.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is a response-format knockout risk: the RFP explicitly says simple Yes/No answers will not be considered sufficient.

requirements.text: All clarification queries must be submitted within the defined timeline and use the template for Q&A provided to the contact provided.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Clarification process requires a specific timeline and Q&A template; missing the clarification window may prevent resolving critical assumptions.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response template provided as part of this RFP to ensure consistency and comparability across all submissions.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The mandatory response template is a separate artifact; non-use could make the response non-comparable or non-compliant.

requirements.text: Pricing must be transparent, structured and clearly itemized, aligned to the requirements of this RFP and the inventory baseline provided.
Vendors must provide foundational setup costs, including landing zone, security, governance and compliance configurations.
Vendors must provide application and infrastructure hosting costs including compute, storage, networking and platform services.
Vendors must provide migration / implementation costs if applicable, including transition or onboarding activities.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Migration execution is listed as out of scope for Phase 0, yet pricing asks for migration/implementation costs if applicable.

requirements.text: Vendors must provide any consulting, support, or managed services costs.
Vendors must provide separate cost views for SAP and non-SAP workloads, ensuring clear visibility into cost drivers across application categories.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Separate SAP versus non-SAP costing is mandatory and easy to miss in pricing details; bundled enterprise pricing may be non-comparable.

requirements.text: All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD).
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Mandatory currency requirement is buried in pricing guidelines; non-USD pricing risks commercial non-compliance.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: All commercial values must be in United States Dollars.

requirements.text: Proposals should include cost increase protections and efficiency gains that lead to a reduction or no more than a reasonable increase over time.
Cost increase protections and efficiency gains should be based on prices being paid and not general discounts off list price.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The RFP rejects generic list-price discounting as the basis for cost protections; pricing must be anchored to prices being paid.

requirements.text: Anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive, and the proposal should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Net 90 is a buried commercial term with explicit negative consequence for shorter terms: less than net 90 will not be considered competitive.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Payment terms expectation is net 90 or better for Kohler.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to provide a high-level overview of their proposed program governance model, including governance structure and engagement model, reporting and review mechanisms, escalation framework, and key roles and responsibilities.
Vendors must ensure that all information received from Kohler is used solely for the purpose of responding to this RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Confidentiality terms are in the appendix rather than the response instructions.

requirements.text: Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This third-party disclosure restriction is in the appendix and may affect subcontractor, hyperscaler, partner, or offshore review workflows.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Prior written consent from Kohler is required before sharing information with any third party.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to ensure that their proposed solutions are capable of complying with applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The appendix restates broad compliance capability expectations but does not enumerate exact regulatory frameworks or data protection obligations.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applies to applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.

ambiguities: The RFP does not state an explicit budget, current annual spend, or committed award value; estimated_deal_size_usd is inferred from scope and complexity.
The RFP does not state a contract term or renewal structure; contract_duration is inferred for the future hosting/business-case opportunity, while Phase 0 itself is only an evaluation exercise.
The key-date table omits the year, but the cover/version date is 29-05-2026; dates are assumed to be in 2026.
Specific certifications and named compliance standards are not listed; the RFP only says evidence of certifications is required and refers vendors to the Vendor Response template Section 4.
Detailed requirements, evaluation weights, scoring structure, and response fields are in the Evaluation Template Excel, which is referenced but not included in the text provided.
The detailed inventory baseline spreadsheet is referenced but not included in the text provided; exact workload counts/sizing cannot be validated from this document alone.
The RFP requires a region strategy and data residency approach but does not state any explicit country, state, sovereignty, or no-offshore processing restriction.
The RFP says detailed migration execution is out of scope for Phase 0, but the pricing section asks for migration/implementation costs if applicable, creating ambiguity on commercial scope.

Most mandatory instructions do not use the words automatic rejection or disqualification; quoted must/required/not sufficient/not competitive clauses have been treated as eliminatory risks where non-compliance would likely make the proposal non-compliant.
hard_disqualifiers: [PAGE 7] Certification evidence is mandatory: the RFP states, "Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards. Evidence of certifications required."
[PAGE 7] DCaaS-specific compliance attestation is mandatory: "DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards."

[PAGE 8] Proposal timing/channel compliance is mandatory: "Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel"; [PAGE 9] lists responses/proposals due to Kohler on 26 June.

[PAGE 8] Template detail is mandatory and simple Yes/No answers are insufficient: "Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple ‘Yes/No’ answers will not be considered sufficient."
[PAGE 9] Vendor Response template use is mandatory: "Vendors are required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response template provided as part of this RFP."
[PAGE 9] Pricing currency is mandatory: "All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD)."

[PAGE 10] Payment terms below net 90 are a commercial knockout risk: "Anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive, and your proposal should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms."
[PAGE 11] Confidentiality restriction is mandatory: "Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler."
compliance_red_flags: [PAGE 7] Evidence of certifications is required, but the specific certifications are not named in the main RFP.
[PAGE 7] DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards, with more detail in the Vendor Response template Section 4.
[PAGE 6] Vendors must address region strategy and data residency approach, but no acceptable regions or residency constraints are defined in this document.
[PAGE 8] Simple Yes/No answers will not be considered sufficient; failure to provide narrative/detail in the template is a high-risk compliance failure.
[PAGE 9] Vendors are required to use the Vendor Response template; non-template responses risk non-compliance.
[PAGE 9] All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in USD.
[PAGE 10] Payment terms below net 90 will not be considered competitive and proposals should contemplate no less than net 90.
[PAGE 11] Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share Kohler information with third parties without prior written consent.
[PAGE 10] Detailed evaluation criteria and weightages are in an external Excel template, not the main RFP text.
[PAGE 11] Kohler reserves the right to modify, amend, withdraw the RFP, request clarification, or accept/reject any proposal without obligation or liability.

similar_deals.key_lesson: Manufacturing domain credibility, client trust, and rapid mobilization were decisive; show a ready-to-start team and sector-specific transition controls.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation

win_intel.win_probability_rationale: Estimated win probability is 36% with medium confidence. Evidence supporting a credible bid: the knowledge base contains an extremely close Project Polaris/Kohler proposal pattern with directly reusable Phase 0 pricing around $279,533, a downstream 36-month modernization benchmark around $30.67M, and a strong solution pattern covering workload placement, landing zones, TCO/FinOps, DR/HA, SAP dependency mapping, rollback gates, and incumbent coexistence. The deal corpus also includes relevant manufacturing wins, especially DEAL-089, where manufacturing credibility and rapid mobilization won a $22M transformation, and DEAL-003, where SAP depth and low-downtime methods won a global manufacturing program. Evidence lowering probability: there is no public procurement award data to validate market pricing or competitor win rates; procurement ground truth is absent. More importantly, the past proposal intelligence identifies Cognizant as a public strategic Kohler partner across data-center operations, cloud platforms, cybersecurity, observability, and advanced cloud management, creating a significant incumbent advantage. The deal corpus also shows two highly relevant manufacturing losses, DEAL-056 and DEAL-100, caused by compliance/local requirement misses, while this RFP contains multiple mandatory knockout items: certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, detailed template completion, USD pricing, net-90 terms, timeline/channel compliance, and confidentiality restrictions. Because Phase 0 is advisory and may favor an independent challenger, the bid is viable, but incumbent position plus compliance risk prevent an above-40% assessment.
capability_gaps: Certification evidence is a gating gap unless supplied in the response: RFP page 7 requires vendors to demonstrate hosting compliance with applicable industry/regional standards and provide evidence of certifications; the supplied intelligence does not confirm which certifications will be attached for cloud, managed hosting, security operations, or regional compliance.

DCaaS compliance attestation is unresolved if we propose a DCaaS scenario: RFP page 7 requires DCaaS providers to explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards; the intelligence provides a DCaaS evaluation approach but not confirmed attestations for specific DCaaS facilities or partners.

Detailed RFP-template compliance is a known execution risk: pages 8-9 require detailed completion of Kohler’s Vendor Response template and state Yes/No answers are insufficient; prior manufacturing losses DEAL-056 and DEAL-100 show our similar pursuits have failed on compliance appendix/local requirement misses rather than technical weakness.

Commercial terms must be confirmed: RFP page 10 states anything below net 90 payment terms will not be competitive; the larger past proposal scenario included net-90, but the Phase 0 commercial package must explicitly carry net-90 and USD-only pricing to avoid a commercial knockout.

Incumbent coexistence must be explicitly solved: the past proposal intelligence identifies Cognizant as a strategic Kohler partner across data-center operations, cloud platforms, AI-driven security/observability, and Cognizant Neuro IT Operations; the RFP requires an operating model, shared responsibility framework, governance, support model, and secure hybrid integration, so a weak or adversarial incumbent-transition model would be a material gap.
recommended_win_themes: Manufacturing-safe hosting transformation: position Project Polaris as a business-continuity and production-risk decision, not a generic cloud assessment; emphasize plant dependencies, maintenance windows, OT/IT boundaries, rollback gates, and manufacturing hypercare.

SAP-aware and performance-protected architecture: make SAP/ERP dependency mapping, application performance baselining, RTO/RPO validation, HA/DR patterns, and low/zero-downtime migration planning central to Phase 0.

Evidence-led cloud/DCaaS/hybrid decision engine: reuse the proven 12-week Polaris Decision Lab / Hosting Option Control Tower pattern with workload-by-workload placement scorecards across public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, plant/edge, hybrid, and SaaS.

Open-book TCO and FinOps transparency: present USD-only commercial models, landing-zone cost guardrails, reserved capacity/licensing optimization, chargeback/showback, and a business case that compares options objectively rather than forcing a cloud-first answer.

Incumbent-compatible operating model: explicitly show how we will coexist with Kohler’s current strategic providers through shared responsibility, service integration, governance, tooling, escalation, security operations, and transition-disruption controls.
risk_factors: No public procurement award data is available, so competitor pricing and award patterns cannot be validated against government contract ground truth; confidence is therefore medium, not high.

Cognizant appears to have a strong incumbent/strategic position with Kohler across data-center operations, cloud platforms, cybersecurity, observability, and advanced cloud management; this materially lowers win probability unless Kohler wants an independent evaluator or challenger perspective.

The current RFP is Phase 0 advisory/evaluation; downstream $15M-$50M+ transformation value is inferred and not guaranteed. Winning Phase 0 may not automatically convert into migration/managed services scope.

Two highly relevant manufacturing transformation losses, DEAL-056 and DEAL-100, were caused by missed local entity/compliance requirements, indicating a recurring pursuit failure mode that could disqualify an otherwise strong technical response.

Mandatory certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, detailed response-template completion, USD pricing, net-90 payment terms, timeline/channel compliance, and confidentiality restrictions create multiple knockout risks.
If we over-position a preferred end-state, such as cloud-only or Azure-first, we may conflict with the RFP’s need to evaluate Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid scenarios objectively.
Kohler’s SAP, VMware, physical server, DR/HA, manufacturing, hospitality, and global-region complexity increases discovery risk and makes unsupported migration assumptions dangerous.
must_fix_before_bidding: Attach a mandatory compliance evidence pack: cloud/security certifications, regional compliance mappings, data residency controls, and, if DCaaS is proposed, facility/provider attestations.

Run a hard-gated compliance review against every RFP instruction: Kohler response template, detailed narrative answers, USD-only pricing, proposal channel/timeline, confidentiality restrictions, net-90 terms, and any local contracting/entity requirements.

Build a named Cognizant/incumbent coexistence model covering RACI, service integration, tooling integration, escalation paths, observability handoffs, transition boundaries, and no-disruption cutover governance.

Price the proposal as two separable options: Phase 0 fixed-price advisory around the prior $279,533 benchmark, plus a clearly optional downstream 36-month modernization case around the prior $30.67M benchmark, with assumptions and exclusions transparent.

Add manufacturing/SAP proof points and named delivery leadership before submission: SAP dependency mapping, VMware/physical server discovery, plant/edge latency considerations, DR/HA baselining, rollback gates, and manufacturing hypercare.

competitors.likelihood_to_bid: high — Wisconsin/Milwaukee job postings for SAP Manufacturing Workstream Manager roles and SAP Product Costing Consultant show relevant manufacturing/SAP hiring in the geography; no public Wisconsin contract win found. Confidence: high on bidding signal, medium on final pursuit decision (job postings + web search).
competitors.predicted_positioning: Accenture will likely position as a Tier 1 global transformation partner for manufacturing, SAP, cloud landing zones, AI-enabled operations, and managed services. Proof points they are likely to cite: Unilever global manufacturing digital twins announced June 16, 2026; Accenture AWS Business Group expansion on June 16, 2026; Google Cloud Gemini Enterprise partnership in May 2026; Microsoft/Avanade Agentic Factory for manufacturing downtime; Databricks enterprise AI partnership on March 17, 2026 (web search). No recent contract wins in Wisconsin were identified; public procurement data is unavailable.
competitors.predicted_price_range_usd: Full transformation/run: likely $24M-$50M; Phase 0 advisory likely materially smaller. No public contract award values are available. Grounding: Accenture Q3 FY2026 revenue $18.7B, operating income $3.2B, operating margin 17.0%; pricing-floor formula = cost / (1 - 0.17) = cost / 0.83 = 1.205x cost. Therefore a $24M-$50M bid implies an internal delivery cost envelope of about $19.9M-$41.5M. Revenue/employee calculation unavailable because employee count was not provided in the supplied sources. Confidence: medium because margin data is strong but public contract price anchors are unavailable.
their_strengths: Strong global enterprise scale and financial stability: Q3 FY2026 revenue $18.7B, operating income $3.2B, operating margin 17.0%, new bookings $19.3B (web search / financial signal).
Recent CPG/manufacturing proof: Unilever multi-year program to scale AI-enabled digital twins across a global manufacturing network, announced June 16, 2026 (web search).
Deep hyperscaler and platform alliances relevant to landing zones and modernization: AWS, Google Cloud, Microsoft/Avanade, and Databricks partnerships all cited in 2026 web intelligence (web search).

Relevant Wisconsin hiring signal: Milwaukee, WI listed for SAP Manufacturing Workstream Manager and SAP Product Costing Consultant roles tied to SAP manufacturing, supply chain, S/4HANA, industrial equipment, smart factories and IoT (job posting intelligence).
their_weaknesses: No recent public Accenture enterprise IT contract win in Wisconsin was found; public procurement data is unavailable (web search + public contract source).
Managed-services demand signal is imperfect: S&P Global noted investor focus on weaker-than-expected bookings, especially Managed Services, despite solid EPS and margins (web search).

Likely premium-price perception: 17.0% Q3 FY2026 operating margin means Accenture must price at roughly 1.205x internal cost to preserve margin; this creates room to undercut with a leaner Phase 0 and fixed-scope commercial model (financial signal).

Their public messaging is heavy on AI/digital manufacturing; Kohler’s RFP is fundamentally a hosting-options evaluation, so they may over-index on transformation narrative unless forced into detailed DCaaS/cloud/hybrid architecture, certifications, DR/HA and operating-model evidence (RFP + web search).

draft.rfp_id: Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
draft.executive_summary:
Kohler’s Project Polaris is not a binary cloud-versus-data-center exercise; it is a resilience, cost, architecture, security, and operating-model decision for a global manufacturer whose enterprise platforms, Wisconsin Data Center, plants, hospitality operations, analytics, connected products, and customer digital experiences must continue to perform while the target state is evaluated. Eric Tabor’s January 2026 priorities for technology-enabled operational and business excellence, productivity, customer value, advanced platforms, and digital engineering reinforce the real purpose of Polaris: choose hosting options that simplify processes, protect production, and create a governed foundation for innovation.

HCLTech proposes a balanced 36-month Polaris Hosting Option Control Tower, beginning with a contractually vendor-neutral, Kohler-owned 12-week Phase 0 Hosting Decision Factory. We will compare public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, plant/edge, hybrid, and SaaS options workload by workload; baseline SAP, ERP, VMware, physical-server, network, security, RTO/RPO, and cost dependencies; design an Azure-first landing zone with hybrid connectivity and guardrails; and build an open-book USD TCO and FinOps model that does not force a cloud-first answer.

The result is an executable business case, certification and compliance evidence matrix, migration-wave plan, DR test blueprint, shared-responsibility RACI, and no-lock-in outputs that Kohler or any downstream provider can execute. Polaris becomes a manufacturing-safe, evidence-led modernization decision with incumbent-compatible controls, performance protection, and transparent economics.
win_themes: Manufacturing-safe hosting transformation: Project Polaris as a business-continuity and production-risk decision with plant dependencies, maintenance windows, OT/IT boundaries, rollback gates, and manufacturing hypercare.

SAP-aware and performance-protected architecture: SAP/ERP dependency mapping, application performance baselining, RTO/RPO validation, HA/DR patterns, and low/zero-downtime migration planning central to Phase 0.

Evidence-led cloud/DCaaS/hybrid decision engine: 12-week Polaris Decision Lab / Hosting Option Control Tower with workload-by-workload placement scorecards across public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, plant/edge, hybrid, and SaaS.
Open-book TCO and FinOps transparency: USD-only commercial models, landing-zone cost guardrails, reserved capacity/licensing optimization, chargeback/showback, and objective option comparison.

Incumbent-compatible operating model: coexistence with Kohler’s current strategic providers through shared responsibility, service integration, governance, tooling, escalation, security operations, and transition-disruption controls.

sections.content:
Polaris must answer a practical business question: which hosting pattern protects Kohler’s production, enterprise performance, customer engagement, and global operations while creating the flexibility required for advanced platforms and digital engineering? Kohler’s environment spans corporate applications, SAP and ERP dependencies, VMware and physical infrastructure, plant and edge connectivity, hospitality operations, analytics, digital channels, current data-center operations, and strategic-provider interfaces. A generic cloud assessment would understate the operational risk.

HCLTech will treat the challenge as manufacturing-safe hosting transformation. We will evaluate plant dependencies, OT/IT boundaries, latency-sensitive integrations, maintenance windows, rollback requirements, RTO/RPO expectations, HA/DR patterns, and manufacturing hypercare before any placement recommendation is made. SAP-aware performance protection will start in Phase 0 through application baselining, dependency mapping, licensing review, and resilience-tier validation.

The second challenge is decision credibility. Kohler needs an evidence-led cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, edge, and SaaS comparison with transparent USD economics, not a partner-led recommendation optimized for downstream pull-through. The third challenge is coexistence: current providers, tools, security operations, escalation paths, and service-management processes must remain stable while Polaris defines the future. Our approach gives Kohler a governed decision framework that protects continuity today and enables a confident hybrid target state tomorrow.

HCLTech proposes the Balanced Polaris Hosting Option Control Tower: a 36-month, co-managed hybrid modernization anchored by a 12-week vendor-neutral Phase 0 Hosting Decision Factory. The first deliverable is Kohler-owned evidence: workload placement scorecards across public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS; a SAP/ERP, VMware, physical-server, network, data, and security dependency model; RTO/RPO and DR test blueprints; and an open-book USD TCO with FinOps guardrails.

The recommended target-state pattern is Azure-first where the evidence supports it, not Azure-only. We will design multi-subscription landing zones with identity integration, environment segregation, data-residency choices, hybrid connectivity, policy controls, observability, backup, security monitoring, and cost guardrails. Workloads that require local continuity, low latency, specialized plant integration, or incumbent-provider control can remain in DCaaS, retained data center, or plant/edge patterns until evidence supports transition.

The modernization path includes migration-wave execution with testing, rollback gates, HA/DR validation, manufacturing hypercare, service integration, and fee-at-risk governance tied to performance, availability, and disruption avoidance. The commercial model is USD-only, net-90 compliant, and priced at $30,667,392 for the 36-month program. Most importantly, the Phase 0 package includes a no-lock-in clause: Kohler can execute the outputs with HCLTech, internally, or through any downstream provider. That neutrality turns Polaris into an objective decision engine.

Delivery begins with a disciplined 12-week Phase 0, then moves only through approved gates into modernization waves. Weeks 1-2 mobilize governance, confirm stakeholders, secure discovery access, align with incumbent providers, and establish the risk log. Weeks 3-5 baseline applications, SAP and ERP dependencies, infrastructure, VMware and physical-server estates, costs, service levels, data flows, network paths, security controls, contracts, and current operational procedures.

Weeks 6-7 run the evidence-led hosting assessment. Each workload receives a placement scorecard covering business criticality, latency, integration complexity, resilience, compliance, licensing, migration effort, run cost, and modernization readiness. Weeks 8-9 produce the target-state blueprint: landing zone, connectivity, identity, segmentation, observability, backup, DR, FinOps, security operations, and service-integration designs. Weeks 10-11 build migration waves, rollback gates, testing plans, manufacturing maintenance-window assumptions, hypercare models, and the open-book TCO. Week 12 delivers the executive decision package, business case, evidence matrix, RACI, DR test blueprint, and next-phase plan.

For the 36-month execution, the 30-FTE blended global delivery and run team will operate through a Control Tower covering SDM, architects, SRE/cloud operations, security, DBA, network, FinOps, and service integration. Each wave proceeds only after performance baselines, RTO/RPO validation, security readiness, rollback approval, and incumbent handoff criteria are met. This gate-based method protects production while building modernization momentum.

HCLTech will staff Polaris with a blended global team sized for advisory credibility and execution continuity. The proposed 30-FTE run and delivery model includes a Service Delivery Manager, program governance lead, enterprise and cloud architects, SAP/ERP dependency specialists, VMware and infrastructure engineers, network architects, security and compliance leads, SRE/cloud operations engineers, database administrators, observability specialists, FinOps analysts, service-integration leads, and manufacturing hypercare coordinators.

The team is organized around the win themes rather than technology silos. Manufacturing-safe transformation is led through plant dependency reviews, OT/IT boundary validation, maintenance-window planning, rollback gates, and hypercare. SAP-aware architecture is led through dependency mapping, performance baselining, HA/DR pattern review, and RTO/RPO validation. Evidence-led option selection is led by the Hosting Decision Factory, using workload placement scorecards and executive decision workshops. Open-book FinOps is led by analysts who compare migration, run, licensing, network, DCaaS, retained data center, support, and cloud consumption costs in USD. Incumbent-compatible operations are led through RACI, tooling, escalation, and service-management integration.

Our credentials for this bid are practical: HCLTech is proposing the same Kohler-specific Polaris Control Tower benchmark reflected in the pursuit knowledge base, including vendor-neutral Phase 0 outputs, Azure-first landing-zone capability, hybrid operations, observability, FinOps, security governance, and co-managed modernization. The team’s mandate is to give Kohler executable evidence, not a predetermined platform answer.

Kohler should choose HCLTech because our proposal preserves choice. Competitors with strong cloud, platform, and transformation alliances can credibly execute migrations, but Polaris first requires an implementation-independent decision that Kohler can trust. Our killer differentiator is a contractually vendor-neutral, Kohler-owned Phase 0 Hosting Decision Factory delivered fixed price, USD-only, net-90 compliant, and supported by a no-lock-in clause. The outputs can be executed by HCLTech, Kohler, an incumbent, or any future provider.

We also protect what matters most during the decision. Polaris will not disrupt manufacturing operations to create an assessment artifact. We baseline performance, validate SAP and ERP dependencies, respect maintenance windows, define rollback gates, map OT/IT boundaries, and design manufacturing hypercare into every migration wave. We pair that production discipline with open-book TCO, FinOps guardrails, reserved-capacity and licensing optimization, chargeback/showback, and objective comparison across cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, edge, and SaaS.

Finally, HCLTech is incumbent-compatible by design. Our governance model defines shared responsibility, tooling integration, security operations interfaces, escalation paths, KPI management, and transition-disruption controls so Kohler can modernize without destabilizing the current ecosystem. We are not asking Kohler to buy a cloud destination first and justify it later. We are offering a defensible decision factory, then a controlled modernization path that advances operational excellence, customer value, and innovation on Kohler’s terms.
